title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21
key_entities: IFS Centre for Tax Analysis in Developing Countries (TaxDev)
summary: This report analyzes the tax expenditure on import goods in Ethiopia from FY 2018/19 to FY 2020/21. The report finds that tax expenditures on certain goods, such as vehicles and animal or vegetable fats, account for a significant share of total tax expenditures. The report also notes a decrease in tax expenditures on second schedule rate goods over the three-year period.

title: September 2022
summary: September 2022

title: Tax Policy Directorate, Ministry of Finance, Ethiopia
summary: Tax Policy Directorate, Ministry of Finance, Ethiopia

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 2
summary: This section provides an overview of the import tax expenditure report for Ethiopia, covering policy rationale, definitions, tax expenditure estimates, and conclusions. The report aims to identify tax expenditures and issues related to tax expenditures. The data is presented in various tables and sections, providing a comprehensive overview of import tax expenditure in Ethiopia.

title: Preface
summary: Preface

title: Contents
summary: Contents

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 3
summary: The report estimates import tax expenditures for the Ethiopian government from FY 2018/19 to FY 2020/21, totaling ETB 120.7 billion. The report uses shipment-level microdata and estimates tax expenditures by category and for each tax type. The report does not account for behavioural or compliance effects and may overstate the true fiscal cost of tax expenditures.

title: Executive summary
summary: Executive summary

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 4
summary: Import tax expenditures in Ethiopia were significantly high in FY 2020/21, with nearly 40% of total expenditures attributed to motor vehicles and animal/vegetable fats. Tax exemptions for capital goods and second schedule prefferential customs rates account for only 2.3% and 1.0% of total expenditures. The value of imports in these categories has significantly increased over the years.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 5
summary: This section describes the methodology and scope of the import tax expenditure report, highlighting the limitations of estimating tax expenditures and the focus on importing taxes. The report builds on previous estimates and aims to provide accurate estimates for federal import tax expenditures during 2018/19, 2019/20, and 2020/21. The report uses shipment-level microdata on customs duty, excise duty, VAT, and surtax collections.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 7
summary: The report on import tax expenditure highlights concerns about transparency in forgoing tax revenue, which could lead to rent-seeking and corruption. The aim is to transform the Ethiopian economy and improve living standards. However, the lack of tax returns from businesses with no liability complicates the process.

title: The intended effects of such tax policy measures and incentives are mainly:
summary: The Ethiopian government uses tax policy measures and incentives to stabilize domestic prices, support local producers, and encourage investment in the manufacturing sector. Incentives include duty-free imports, tax holidays, and preferential tax treatments. The goal is to accelerate economic development and improve living standards.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 8
summary: This section explains the approach to estimating tax expenditures, focusing on the removal of tax expenditures and deviations from standard rates. The report uses the revenue forgone method and considers deviations from standard tax rates for customs duty, surtax, and other exemptions. The goal is to determine positive import tax expenditures, excluding negative tax expenditures that arise from adjustments in the benchmark tax system.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 9
summary: Ethiopia uses a tiered customs duty system with differing rates for goods imported from COMESA countries and those with other trade agreements, based on the 2017 tariff book.

title: Customs duty
summary: In the Ethiopian import tax system, customs duty and other taxes are charged sequentially. Tax expenditures on import taxes can lead to higher collection of domestic taxes, such as VAT and income taxes.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 10
summary: This section discusses the import tax expenditure report for Ethiopia, covering goods and services not exempt from Value-Added Tax (VAT) and its implementation, including a national invoice-credit system.

title: The benchmark tax system for customs duty is defined as follows:
summary: The benchmark tax system for customs duty is defined as a percentage of the dutypaying value, which includes shipment value, transport charges, and other costs. The customs duty rate varies between 0% and 35% depending on the type and level of processing of the imported goods. Final consumer goods are typically charged at higher rates.

title: Excise on imports
summary: This section discusses tax exemptions for certain international organizations and individuals, including duty-free imports for diplomatic personnel and bilateral trade agreements with Sudan.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 11
summary: The Ministry of Finance has amended the surtax payment requirements for certain imported goods, excluding items such as groundnuts, potatoes, and machinery from surtax payments.

title: The benchmark system for excise on imports is defined as follows:
summary: The benchmark system for excise on imports sets tax rates on certain goods, including luxury items and goods with negative externalities. Excise tax is applied to 16 specific categories of goods. Excise rates are set by Proclamation No. 1186/2020.

title: VAT on imports
summary: This section discusses VAT on imports, highlighting excise-free privileges for specific organizations and individuals, benchmark excise tax rates, and the tax base and unit of taxation for excise on imports.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 12
summary:
Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 12
 Ministry of Finance, Ethiopia

title: Surtax on imports
summary: This section discusses the surtax on imports, its implications, and the deductibility of VAT expenditures. It also explains the VAT exemption for international aid organisations and diplomatic imports. The VAT tax rate is 15%, except for certain exemptions.

title: The benchmark system for VAT on imports is defined as follows:
summary: The benchmark system for VAT on imports includes certain agricultural inputs and basic food items, excluding most vegetables and other specific categories.

title: Customs duty tax expenditures are given by
summary: This section explains how to calculate tax expenditures, including indirect effects, and defines the surtax system in Ethiopia. It highlights the complexities of the system due to sequential taxation and exemptions. The section provides a general framework for estimating tax expenditures and understanding the surtax base.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 13
summary: This section explains the tax expenditure report for import taxes in Ethiopia from FY 2018/19 to 2020/21, focusing on the surtax mechanism and its tax base. The surtax mechanism has no indirect effects on other taxes due to its position in the tax sequence. The report aims to provide insights into the country's tax policies.

title: The benchmark tax system for surtax on imports is defined as follows:
summary: The benchmark tax system for surtax on imports is defined as follows:

title: Finally, for surtax, the tax expenditure is
summary:
The section discusses the tax expenditure for surtax in relation to VAT, with details on its calculation.
It focuses on the tax base, which includes import value, customs duty, and excise tax.
The section also mentions the calculation of Internal Earnings VAT.

title: For VAT, the tax expenditure is computed analogously as
summary: This section explains how to compute tax expenditure for Value-Added Tax (VAT), outlining similar calculations for excise taxes and utilizing variables with superscripts for excise taxes.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 14
summary: The import tax expenditure report has an issue due to the transition between customs IT systems, potentially leading to overestimation of tax expenditures. The impact is estimated to be up to ETB 30.6 billion for FY 2018/19 and up to ETB 1.9 billion for FY 2019/20. This affects the interpretation of the FY 2018/19 results and trend analysis between FY 2018/19 and FY 2019/20.

title: Similarly, excise tax expenditures are computed as
summary: Excise tax expenditures are computed based on customs duty tax expenditures due to exemptions and reduced rates. These expenditures arise when the actual rate charged is zero or smaller than the benchmark duty rate, respectively. The calculation includes the cost of imports, insurance, and freight, as well as the indirect effect on other import taxes.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 15
summary: The tax expenditure report for FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 in Ethiopia shows an increase in tax expenditures as a percentage of GDP and imports, indicating a rise in tax expenditures despite a relatively small increase in imports.

title: The data contain shipment-level information on:
summary: The report uses shipment-level microdata from the Ethiopian Customs Commission at the eight-digit HS level from FY 2018/19 to FY 2020/21, combining data from ASYCUDA++ and eCMS systems.

summary: This section discusses the tax expenditures in various forms, including duties, excise duty, VAT, and surtax, in Ethiopia between FY 2018/19 and FY 2020/21. The overall tax expenditure estimate for FY 2020/21 was ETB 120.7 billion. The tax expenditures were made up of forgone customs duty, excise duty, VAT on imports, and surtax.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 17
summary: This section of the import tax expenditure report discusses the large year-on-year variation in tax expenditures across different categories, which is likely linked to government policy changes, including exemptions and new tax laws. The report highlights the varying contributions of vehicle imports, animal fat, and non-electrical machinery to overall tax expenditures. Additionally, it provides a comparison of tax expenditures across different years, showing differences in the nominal values of imports and total tax expenditures.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 21
summary: The report discusses import tax expenditure in Ethiopia from FY 2018/19 to 2020/21, with a focus on methodology differences and varying estimates. An initial estimate puts tax expenditures at ETB 38.9 billion, while alternative methods provide lower estimates. The use of benchmark rates and data sources affects the final calculations.

title: Table 4.4. Different estimates of total tax expenditures (in ETB billions)
summary: This table discusses the definition of tax expenditures using different methods: using standard rates by the Ethiopian Customs Commission or the Ministry of Finance's previous definition for 2018/19.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 22
summary: This section reports on customs duty tax expenditures in Ethiopia for FY 2018/19 to 2020/21, highlighting their relative contribution to GDP, imports, and exemptions. Exemptions accounted for a growing share of customs tax expenditures, and indirect effects of customs expenditure on other taxes also changed significantly over the period. The data is based on calculations by the Ethiopian Customs Commission.

title: Higher order indirect effects
summary: This section discusses higher order indirect effects, specifically customs duty tax expenditures from FY 2018/19 to FY 2020/21. The expenditures were significant, increasing to ETB 30 billion in FY 2018/19 and ETB 23 billion in FY 2020/21. The amount paid in customs duty has steadily decreased over the years.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 23
summary: This section of the report discusses the share of customs duty tax expenditures in relation to imports, GDP, and total trade tax expenditures from FY 2018/19 to FY 2020/21. The share of customs duty tax expenditures on imports declined, while the share of customs duty expenditures on second schedule imports also decreased. The decline was due to a reduction in expenditure on second schedule imports.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 29
summary: This report provides a summary of the import tax expenditure in Ethiopia from FY 2018/19 to FY 2020/21. Between FY 2018/19 and FY 2020/21, there has been a significant decline in the share of vehicle imports, while a notable increase was observed in the share of prepared foods and beverages. The report highlights year-on-year fluctuations in excise tax expenditures across different commodity categories.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 31
summary: The report highlights the high tax expenditures on motor vehicle imports in Ethiopia, particularly due to 'prohibitive' tax rates, with total expenditures more than doubling in 2020/21. Removing tax exemptions on vehicles could potentially gain significant revenue. The government's reform raised tax rates on some used vehicles, but actual collections barely increased. This led to a large increase in tax expenditures, mainly due to larger indirect customs duty expenditures.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 37
summary: The report discusses the decline of capital imports and second schedule imports as a share of surtax expenditures in Ethiopia, from FY 2018/19 to FY 2020/21, reflecting the government's policy to phase out second schedule privileges. The majority of surtax expenditures have increased to non-capital imports and pre- second schedule shipments. This has reduced the importance of second schedule imports to 1.9% in FY 2020/21.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 40
summary: This report highlights the need to improve the quality and comprehensiveness of tax expenditure reports, particularly for domestic taxes, to gain a full understanding of tax revenues. Import tax expenditures are significant, with large averages in categories like motor vehicles and animal and vegetable fats. The government should regularly scrutinize tax expenditures to ensure they align with objectives.

title: Import tax expenditure report: FY 2018/19 - 2020/21 42
summary: The report discusses the negative tax expenditures in Ethiopia, mainly related to customs duty expenditures, which are small and mostly due to outdated tariff books. The report excludes these expenditures from main estimates and focuses on positive tax expenditures. A small minority of shipments showed higher customs duty or tax rates than under the benchmark tax system.

title: F. Supplementary tables
summary:
This section categorizes commodities into various groups based on the Harmonized System (HS) codes, which are used for international trade classification.

The table provides a detailed list of commodity categories, HS codes, and corresponding chapter ranges. It helps to simplify the classification of goods for international trade.

The table consists of 21 categories, with the last category covering all remaining HS codes not explicitly listed.
